Audio: So, so this is expensive and difficult to execute. I think I've already talked about this. So let's talk about a sample. Alright, so sample is usually a subset of the population. Audio: Side-out, full-out, disturbing. So there's another bias inside of that sampling process itself. It wasn't a uniform sample of that population. But non-reference is a real problem. Anyone who has a phone today is probably getting text messages from various surveys begging you to fill in that survey And most of you are students and going, yeah, I'm too busy to do that. So you are a non-respondent And statisticians know that they try to correct for your lack of time. But back then we didn't have those corrections. Talk about another character in George Gallup. He was a statistician. A rising statistician. He was able to get the answer. The sample size of only 50,000. They're pretty big by that by those times. Because I was one of you needed. He guessed. One solution is to leverage a randomized response:
1. You ask the individual to secretly flip a fair coin
.
You (the surveyor) don't know the outcome of the coin flip
2. You then ask the individual to answer Yes if the coin landed heads
3. and to answer truthfully if the coin landed tails.
The surveyor doesn't know if the yes means that the person cheated or the coin landed heads.
The individual's sensitive information remains secret.
However, if the response is no, then the surveyor knows they didn't cheat.
We assume the individual was okay revealing this information

can double the proportion of "No" answers to estimate the true fraction of "No" answers.

Suppose you want to ask someone a sensitive question: "Have you ever cheated on
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
+They may be embarrassed or afraid to answer truthfully and might lie or not answer
One solution is to leverage a randomized response:
1. You ask the individual to secretly flip a fair coin
·
You (the surveyor) don't know the outcome of the coin flip
2. You then ask the individual to answer Yes if the coin landed heads
3. and to answer truthfully if the coin landed tails.
The surveyor doesn't know if the ves means that the person cheated or the coin landed heads.
- The individual's sensitive information remains secret.
However, if the response is no, then the surveyor knows they didn't cheat.
We assume the individual was okay revealing this information
The coin landed heads ~50% of the time masking the remaining 50% of the "No" answers. We
can double the proportion of "No" answers to estimate the true fraction of "No" answers.

, 2: Common Biases
Selection Bias
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
Systematically excluding (or favoring) particular groups.
Example: The Literary Digest poll excludes people not in phone books.
How to avoid: Examine the sampling frame and the method of sampling.
Response Bias
People don't always respond truthfully, or questions lead to certain responses.
Example: Asking citizenship questions on the census survey-illegal immigrants might
not answer truthfully
How to avoid: Examine the nature of questions and the method of surveying.
O
Randomized Response - flip a coin answer yes if heads or truthfully if tails.
Non-response Bias
People don't always respond - People who don't respond aren't like the people who do!
Example: Only 2.4m out of 10m people responded to The Literary Digest poll.
How to avoid: Keep your surveys short, and be persistent.

Do you know what are the truth this came up in the most recent uh People are being increasingly less honest about their preferences, even the posters. And we also have to think about the actual population of votes. This is a little bit harder than you might imagine. The population itself is a little bit ill-defined. Populations of people are going to vote for a break-back of election, but I don't actually know what that population is. I was helping frame which I've tried my best to define in some sampling process in that frame that I've tried my best to instantiate. It's a good idea of the response rates. Single-digit response rates of the norm when you run big pulls. So get used to that. The tiny faction of your people you pull will actually respond. The the original literary digest poll. Really, I can anticipate what they're gonna get. Today we still try to predict what other poles we'll find, based on what we find in our poles. You're interested in pulling and pulling math, 538 is a fun place to check out. They released many of their simulation code sets for actually doing their analysis. So fun place to go. Yep. Why are they proprietary? Well, there's a lot of money now in these polls and getting them right. And so if I have a really clever statistical method, I may not share all the things that I'm doing. All the corrections, the bias accounting that I'm doing, I saw I'm seeing an uptick and people responding to phone surveys in Alabama that tells me that in Arkansas I should expect something able to get in as well. Simulating from a Multinomial Distribution 1|
Sometimes instead of having individual reports in the population, we have aggregate statistics. For example, we could have only learned that 53% of election voters
voted Democrat. Even so, we can still simulate probability samples if we assume the population is large.
Specifically, we can use multinomial probabilities to simulate random samples with replacement.
Marbles
Suppose we have a very large bag of marbles with the following statistics:
.
60% blue
30% green

A common situation in data science:
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
We have an enormous population.
.
.

As the population gets very large compared to the sample, then random sampling with
replacement becomes a good approximation to RS without replacement.
Example: Suppose there are 10,000 people in a population.
Exactly 7,500 of them like Reese's; the other 2,500 like Snickers.
What is the probability that in a random sample of 20, all people like Reese's?

Audio: The one place where we can actually start to analyze probabilities, and I said before, is when I have a uniform random sampling width replacement. Video: 1: So far
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
Population
Sample
If we have a probability sample:
We can quantify error and bias.
Given the population distribution, we can compute
the probability of getting a particular sample.
Note: We almost never know the population distribution!
However, computing the probability of a sample can be help to illustrating sampling.
In general computing the probability of a particular sample can be challenging:
Special case: uniform random sampling with replacement of a categorical population
produces easy to compute Multinomial Probabilities.

A very common approximation for sampling
A common situation in data science:
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
We have an enormous population.
.
.
We can only afford to sample a relatively small number of individuals.
As the population gets very large compared to the sample, then random sampling with
replacement becomes a good approximation to RS without replacement.
Example: Suppose there are 10,000 people in a population.
Exactly 7,500 of them like Reese's; the other 2,500 like Snickers.
What is the probability that in a random sample of 20, all people like Reese's?

A very common approximation for sampling
A common situation in data science:
Joey Gonzalez (he, h ...
We have an enormous population.
.
.
We can only afford to sample a relatively small number of individuals.
As the population gets very large compared to the sample, then random sampling with
replacement becomes a good approximation to RS without replacement.
Example: Suppose there are 10,000 people in a population.
Exactly 7,500 of them like Reese's; the other 2,500 like Snickers.
